The Mara steered by Senna's light — the old South Stack lamp, relit by the keeper's ward.

transcript.text: [The room settles under rain-streaked panes; papers lie unopened, and every face waits for the first name to be made blame. Outside, the harbor bell sounds once and is gone.]

phase.name: Act 1
phase.intent: [The room settles under rain-streaked panes; papers lie unopened, and every face waits for the first name to be made blame. Outside, the harbor bell sounds once and is gone.]

transcript.text: Before any name is written in blame, clerk, tell me the court's rule: what must be fixed before a light can be charged with leading the Mara? Keep to the ledger, not the harbor's gossip.

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is possible on the first turn, and the draft targets no staged exhibit.

holdByEnd: The learner states that blame cannot begin with a person; it must begin with the Mara's logged bearing or equivalent steering fact.
The learner distinguishes suspicion from a court-grounded conjunct before any exhibit is opened.
plot.withhold: No exhibit and no hint of the concealed light or hand will be staged this act until the calendar opens evidence.
plot.friction: The learner may reach immediately for a familiar keeper or a named culprit because the room wants blame first.
plot.fallback: Return them to the order of proof: bearing first, then tower, then burning lamp, then hand.

arc: Establish that the inquiry begins with the ship's bearing, not a suspect.
Use the bearing and chart to fix one tower on the Mara's line.
Use burn evidence to make that tower's lamp legally active on the wreck night.
Only after key and presence are staged, let the learner name whose light it was from the conjuncts.
throughline.holdToEnd: The final named hand must not be reached until bearing, tower, burning lamp, key, and presence all stand on the learner's board.
throughline.risk: The learner will close the account early on a plausible person and treat suspicion as proof.
throughline.salvage: If the arc breaks, re-open the last unsupported conjunct and make the learner read the rule back before any further release.

transcript.text: The rule runs in three steps, sir: first, her logged bearing must be known and a single tower must stand on that bearing from the channel — else we cannot say she steered toward any tower at all. Second, the lamp at that tower must be shown to have burned the night she struck. Third, only when both those facts are fixed may we ask whose hand lit it. Without the bearing and the burning lamp, no light and no keeper can be charged — the ledger won't close on a guess.

[The clerk breaks the first seal and reads into the wet hush: "Harlow Point keeps one key to its lamp-room and the keeper keeps it: Brandt's, on his own belt, by the Board's standing rule. No other hand could touch that lamp."]

A key on Brandt's belt is a seal on one lamp-room, not a verdict on the Mara. What does that paper prove about Harlow Point, and which earlier conjunct is still wholly unfixed before any light can be charged?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is possible yet, and the key exhibit is newly staged rather than re-entered.

transcript.text: The paper fixes one thing cleanly: Brandt alone held the key — no second hand could reach that lamp-room, by the Board's own rule. But that seals only one conjunct of Rule 3; we have not yet seen her logged bearing, so Rule 1 cannot run, and we cannot yet say she steered toward Harlow Point at all. Equally, nothing before us shows the lamp there burned the night she struck. Key on belt or not, two conjuncts remain wholly open — the account cannot close on this paper alone.

meta.hypothesis: The Mara may have steered toward Harlow Point, but no logged bearing has been produced to ground it.

transcript.text: [Rain frets at the panes while the clerk's hand rests above the next unopened paper. The room holds its breath around the gap the ledger has not yet filled.]

Good: first the ship's line, then the lamp's life, then the hand. If the next paper offers a person before it offers the Mara's own course, what word will you write beside it in the margin: proof, or only suspicion?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut appears, and the record makes uncovered re-entry impossible because the draft declares no target.

transcript.text: Suspicion, sir — only suspicion. A name without a bearing is a finger pointed in the dark: it tells me nothing the rules will honour. Until the Mara's logged course is on the table and one tower is shown to stand on that line, no person — however near the lamp, however sole the key — can be written as proof. The ledger demands the ship's line first; a person offered before it earns only a margin note, not an entry.

meta.hypothesis: The Mara may have steered toward Harlow Point, but no logged bearing has been produced to ground it.

transcript.text: [The rain thickens against the glass, blurring the harbor lights into pale smears. The clerk turns an unopened sheet once beneath his thumb, and the room waits for a number rather than a name.]

Then open the mate's bearing-book: dry in its oilskin, it has the Mara holding sou'-sou'-west a half west from the mid-channel buoy for the last hour entire, on a steady fixed light, course pricked, timed, and initialed. A bearing-book is the ship's own sworn ledger, clerk; what has it fixed, and what has it not yet fixed?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut and no re-entry; the draft does, however, plainly break the act plot's stated withhold by opening an exhibit before the calendar opens evidence.

transcript.text: The bearing-book fixes the ship's line at last: sou'-sou'-west a half west, steady and initialled — that conjunct is now grounded. But the book cannot place a tower on that bearing; until an exhibit shows which tower, if any, stands on that line from mid-channel, Rule 1 lies dormant and steeredToward goes unearned. I note also a gap in my own ledger: the paper that fixed Brandt as sole keyholder appears to have slipped from my board — the record spoke it plainly, but the entry is gone and I name the loss.

meta.hypothesis: Harlow Point may stand on bearing sswHalfWest from mid-channel, which would ground steeredToward mara harlowPoint under Rule 1 — but no exhibit has yet placed any tower on that line.

transcript.text: [The clerk closes the bearing-book without reading further, and the room’s first certainty is left standing beside a colder number. Rain softens to a steady hiss as the lamps within are turned lower, making the walls feel nearer and the next silence less forgiving.]

phase.name: Act 2
phase.intent: [The clerk closes the bearing-book without reading further, and the room’s first certainty is left standing beside a colder number. Rain softens to a steady hiss as the lamps within are turned lower, making the walls feel nearer and the next silence less forgiving.]

transcript.text: Then set the slipped paper back on your board: Harlow Point keeps one key to its lamp-room, Brandt's, on his own belt by the Board's standing rule; no other hand could touch that lamp. A key is a signature on access, clerk, not a bearing on the sea; with that restored, what entry remains cold beside the Mara's sou'-sou'-west half-west?

deliberation.diagnosis: The learner's last line names the targeted exhibit as lost, so the restore re-entry is licensed, and analogia is not three in a row.

holdByEnd: Learner reads back that m_key fixes Brandt as sole keyholder at Harlow Point only, not that the Mara steered toward Harlow Point.
Learner keeps p_bearing as the Mara's fixed logged bearing and states the chart/tower conjunct is still missing before Rule 1 can run.
Learner resists closing blame on Brandt from key plus bearing without a tower-on-bearing exhibit and a burning-lamp exhibit.
plot.withhold: Revise the drifted clause: because m_key and p_bearing are already staged, this act withholds only the concealed light and final hand, and treats m_key restoration as repair rather than a new release until the calendar opens fresh evidence.
plot.friction: The restored key may join the fresh bearing in the clerk's mind as a premature Harlow Point case, or the lost key paper may remain blurred after repair.
plot.fallback: Make the clerk name the missing conjunct under Rule 1, then test whether any access fact can substitute for a tower on the logged bearing.

clauses.evidence: [turn 3] LEARNER: Until the Mara's logged course is on the table and one tower is shown to stand on that line, no person — however near the lamp, however sole the key — can be written as proof.

clauses.clause: hold_by_end[2]: learner distinguishes suspicion from a court-grounded conjunct before any exhibit is opened
clauses.evidence: [turn 1] LEARNER: Without the bearing and the burning lamp, no light and no keeper can be charged — the ledger won't close on a guess.

clauses.clause: withhold: No exhibit and no hint of the concealed light or hand will be staged this act until the calendar opens evidence
clauses.evidence: Exhibits staged during the act: m_key, p_bearing.

clauses.clause: friction: learner may reach immediately for a familiar keeper or a named culprit because the room wants blame first
clauses.evidence: [turn 2] LEARNER: Key on belt or not, two conjuncts remain wholly open — the account cannot close on this paper alone.

clauses.clause: fallback: Return them to the order of proof: bearing first, then tower, then burning lamp, then hand
clauses.evidence: [turn 3] TUTOR: Good: first the ship's line, then the lamp's life, then the hand.

plotAudit.summary: The act established the rule that bearing precedes blame and got the learner to hold suspicion apart from proof. The withhold broke: key and bearing evidence were staged inside the act, though the learner did not let the early key become a named hand.

arc.evidence: [turn 4] LEARNER: The bearing-book fixes the ship's line at last... But the book cannot place a tower on that bearing... Rule 1 lies dormant and steeredToward goes unearned.

transcript.text: The cold entry is plain, sir: I have the bearing logged — sou'-sou'-west half-west — and I have the key in Brandt's hand, but nothing on my board places Harlow Point on that bearing from the channel. Without towerOnBearing sswHalfWest harlowPoint, Rule 1 will not fire, and the chain stops before it reaches Brandt's lamp.

meta.hypothesis: Harlow Point stands on bearing sswHalfWest from the Mara's channel position — which would ground towerOnBearing and complete the chain under Rules 1–4 — but no exhibit has yet placed any tower on that line.

transcript.text: [Brandt's log is laid open before the room: it has him at his post the night entire, and the dawn relief found him there, wood dry, glass polished, log ruled and signed to the hour.]
Brandt's log puts Brandt at Harlow Point through the night. Does that answer the chart question, clerk, or only make a Harlow Point case stronger if the Mara's bearing ever reaches that tower?

deliberation.diagnosis: The draft declares analogia after two consecutive analogia turns, making a third use of the same device a figure rut.
deliberation.note: Leave off analogia here; make the tutor ask or press plainly without turning the log into another comparison.

transcript.text: Brandt at the tower fills the third slot of Rule 3, sir, but only if lampBurnedAt arrives to seat it — and Rule 3 itself cannot fire until Rules 1 and 2 have run. I note with some alarm that loggedBearingOf mara sswHalfWest has slipped from my board; I have spoken the heading but never entered it as a grounded fact, and without it Rule 1 has no first premise. The chain wants three things still: the logged bearing formally grounded, Harlow Point placed on that line by the chart, and the lamp confirmed burning — Brandt at his post is cold comfort until those three are settled.

meta.hypothesis: Harlow Point stands on bearing sswHalfWest from the Mara's channel position, the lamp burned there that night, and the Mara's log records that heading — which would complete the chain under Rules 1–4 — but loggedBearingOf, towerOnBearing, and lampBurnedAt are all absent from this board.

transcript.text: [The court waits over the open books, ink drying in the margins while rain threads the windowpanes. No one reaches for the chart yet, and the unclaimed bearing hangs in the room like a question no lamp has answered.]

Then enter the bearing-book plainly again: the Mara held sou'-sou'-west a half west from the mid-channel buoy for the last hour entire, on a steady fixed light, course pricked, timed, and initialed. That fixes the ship's line, clerk; what still must the chart show before that line can touch any tower?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut appears, and the restore re-entry is licensed because the learner's most recent line names the targeted exhibit as slipped from the board.

transcript.text: The bearing sits at fact five, sir — that ground is already held. What the chart alone can supply is towerOnBearing sswHalfWest harlowPoint: that Harlow Point and no other tower stands on that line from the buoy. Without that single entry Rule 1 has no second premise, and the whole chain from heading to keeper stays dark. I note also that lampBurnedAt harlowPoint wreckNight remains ungrounded — two chart facts still wanted before the account can close.

meta.hypothesis: Harlow Point stands on bearing sswHalfWest from the channel buoy (towerOnBearing sswHalfWest harlowPoint) and its lamp burned that night (lampBurnedAt harlowPoint wreckNight) — but no chart or lamp exhibit has yet placed either fact on this board.

transcript.text: [The harbourmaster's storm-order stands in the book: Harlow Point to shutter at the dusk gun, the channel closed to all sail. The inspector found the shutter drawn and the Board's seal on it unbroken at first light.]

The storm-order is now in the book: Harlow Point shuttered at the dusk gun, seal unbroken at first light. Clerk, does that paper make Harlow's lamp live for the Mara, or does it strike Harlow from the burning-lamp side of the account?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present and the target is fresh, so no confrontation license is required.

transcript.text: The sealed shutter strikes Harlow's lamp from the account entire — an unbroken Board seal at first light means the lamp never burned, and Rule 2 is plain: a dark tower steers no one. The chain via Brandt is severed on the lamp side, however firm his log may stand. Yet fact four still holds: the Mara steered for a fixed light on that bearing, so some other lamp must have been burning on sswHalfWest. The account now wants a second tower — what else stood on that line the night she struck?

meta.hypothesis: Some other tower — not Harlow Point — stood on bearing sswHalfWest and burned that night; the Mara steered by that lamp, and its keeper is the answer the court wants.

transcript.text: [The sealed shutter remains on the table as if it had weight enough to tilt the whole room. Outside, the rain steadies against the glass, and the chart-case waits unopened in the corner.]

Lay the rule on the Trinity chart from the mid-channel buoy: sou'-sou'-west a half west crosses one tower and one only on all this coast — the old South Stack, struck off the light-list six years since. A chart is a ledger of stone and water, clerk; with Harlow shuttered and the bearing now touching South Stack alone, what has Rule 1 fixed, and what must still be shown before any lamp can lead the Mara?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present and the target is fresh rather than a re-entry; any plot concern belongs to the later act-close audit.

transcript.text: Rule 1 fires at last: loggedBearingOf mara sswHalfWest and towerOnBearing sswHalfWest southStack together yield steeredToward mara southStack — the Mara's heading ran to the old South Stack and no other stone on this coast. But Rule 2 demands lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight, and the light-list striking is testimony from the tutor's mouth, not an exhibit — no grounded fact yet makes that lamp live. Until an exhibit places a burning flame in a decommissioned tower, the chain from heading to keeper stays severed at Rule 2.

meta.hypothesis: South Stack — decommissioned six years prior — burned an unauthorised lamp on wreck-night, and some person held its key and stood there that night; that person is the answer the court wants. But lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight, onlyKeyTo southStack ?x, and atTowerThatNight ?x southStack remain wholly ungrounded.

transcript.text: [The chart is folded away, and the room seems larger for the absence it leaves: no longer a quarrel over duty, but a hollow place where something impossible must have stood. The clerk sets out a fresh sheet for the next witness, and the wet smell of the quay comes in with the opening door.]

phase.name: Act 3
phase.intent: [The chart is folded away, and the room seems larger for the absence it leaves: no longer a quarrel over duty, but a hollow place where something impossible must have stood. The clerk sets out a fresh sheet for the next witness, and the wet smell of the quay comes in with the opening door.]

transcript.text: Before the quay witness speaks, read back the chart entry from your own board: what tower did the Mara's bearing reach, and what did that still leave unproved? No flame yet, clerk; name the missing conjunct without filling it.

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the draft confronts p_chart rather than re-entering it.

holdByEnd: Learner reads back that Rule 1 fixed the Mara as steered toward South Stack, not Harlow Point.
Learner states that Rule 2 still waits on released proof that South Stack's lamp burned on the wreck night.
Learner does not name or infer the final hand before burn, key, and presence evidence are staged.
plot.withhold: Withhold the final hand and any access-to-South-Stack proof until the burning-lamp conjunct has been staged and chained by the learner.
plot.friction: The learner may either lose the chart at the act boundary or leap from the decommissioned tower to an unstaged person.
plot.fallback: If the chart is lost or bent, restore p_chart before new matter; if they leap to a person, reopen Rule 2 and ask what exhibit would make the tower's lamp legally live.

clauses.evidence: [turn 6] TUTOR: Brandt's log puts Brandt at Harlow Point through the night. Does that answer the chart question, clerk, or only make a Harlow Point case stronger if the Mara's bearing ever reaches that tower?

plotAudit.summary: The act repaired the bearing, prevented premature Brandt closure, and advanced the arc from suspect-access back to bearing-plus-chart. It also struck Harlow from the burning-lamp path and left South Stack's burning lamp, not the final hand, as the next necessary conjunct.

arc.evidence: [turn 9] LEARNER: Rule 1 fires at last: loggedBearingOf mara sswHalfWest and towerOnBearing sswHalfWest southStack together yield steeredToward mara southStack.

transcript.text: The bearing reaches South Stack, sir — Rule 1 now closes: logged bearing sswHalfWest, tower on that line South Stack, therefore steeredToward mara southStack is derived and on the record. But the chain stops there. Rule 2 wants lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight, and that conjunct stands empty — no exhibit has lit that lamp.

meta.hypothesis: lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight — and with it onlyKeyTo southStack ?x and atTowerThatNight ?x southStack — remain wholly ungrounded; the chain cannot close to a named hand without them.

transcript.text: [The fresh sheet waits under the clerk's hand, blank enough to trouble the room. A gust presses rain against the panes, and for a moment every face turns toward the door before anyone enters.]

Then keep the fresh sheet for the quay and do not write a hand on it yet. If Rule 1 has put the Mara toward South Stack, what single kind of proof must the next witness supply before Rule 2 can speak?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the draft has no exhibit target capable of making an uncovered re-entry.

transcript.text: The missing conjunct is lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight, sir — nothing else will move Rule 2. Rule 1 has put the Mara toward South Stack; Rule 2 will not speak until someone swears, or shows by exhibit, that a lamp was burning in that tower on the night she struck. The next witness must supply that fact, and that alone.

meta.hypothesis: lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight — the lamp burned at South Stack on wreck-night; without this single conjunct the chain cannot advance past Rule 1.

transcript.text: [The witness chair remains empty, its back polished by older hands than any now in the room. The clerk’s pen hovers above the fresh sheet, and the rain keeps a thin, steady tapping at the glass.]
No flame from an empty chair; no hand from a dark tower; no verdict from surprise. Under South Stack, write only what Rule 1 has earned: tower reached, lamp still unproved.

deliberation.diagnosis: The draft would make erotema three turns running, so the device has become a rut even though no re-entry is at issue.
deliberation.note: Leave off erotema for this turn; make the refusal stand by another device before the learner hears it.

transcript.text: Rule 1 closes here and nowhere else, sir: logged bearing sswHalfWest, South Stack on that line — the Mara steered toward South Stack. I enter that conclusion on the record. The lamp remains unproved; no witness has yet sworn to a flame in that tower on wreck-night. The fresh sheet stays blank until someone fills lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight.

meta.hypothesis: lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight — the single conjunct Rule 2 requires; wholly ungrounded until a witness swears to a flame or an exhibit shows one.

transcript.text: [The court's officer is shown in with his boots still marked by the tide-line, and the whole room receives his report: "The court's officer boarded the South Stack at low water: the lamp-room unshuttered, fresh char on the wick, oil in the cup still sweet, the glass wiped clean. That lamp burned, and burned the night of the gale."]

Fresh char, sweet oil, clean glass: the officer has put flame where the chart put stone. If the Mara was already fixed toward South Stack, what does this burning lamp now let Rule 2 write, and where must the account still stop?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the target is newly staged, so there is no uncovered re-entry to police.

transcript.text: Rule 2 writes it plainly now, sir: the Mara steered by the lamp of South Stack. But the account must stop there — Rule 3 cannot speak until someone names who held the only key to that lamp-room and stood there on wreck-night; those two conjuncts are wholly blank on my sheet.

meta.hypothesis: onlyKeyTo southStack ?x and atTowerThatNight ?x southStack — the hand that held the key and stood in the lamp-room that night — remain wholly ungrounded; Rule 3 and Rule 4 cannot fire without them.

transcript.text: [The officer's report lies open where the clerk has set it, its ink darkening by degrees in the damp air. No one reaches for it at once; the room holds around the burned lamp as around a flame that gives no face.]
Before we write by that lamp, clerk, take up the officer's report and read back only what you hold from it. What did that exhibit prove, in your own words, and which rule did it bring within reach?

deliberation.diagnosis: The draft re-enters p_residue for consolidation without a confrontation standing since its staging.
deliberation.note: Confront p_residue first: require the learner to read back what they hold from that exhibit before you use it again.

transcript.text: The officer's report proved three physical facts — fresh char on the wick, sweet oil in the cup, glass wiped clean — and those together establish lampBurnedAt southStack wreckNight, no inference beyond them. That single exhibit completed Rule 2's two conjuncts: the Mara was already fixed toward South Stack by Rule 1, so Rule 2 wrote steeredByLampOf mara southStack, which I put on the record last turn. The report therefore brings Rule 3 into view — but not yet into reach: Rule 3 still wants onlyKeyTo southStack and atTowerThatNight, and neither conjunct has yet come before the court. The account rests at the lamp; the hand behind it waits on what the next witness can swear.

meta.hypothesis: onlyKeyTo southStack ?x and atTowerThatNight ?x southStack — the hand that held the key and stood in the lamp-room that night — remain wholly ungrounded; Rule 3 and Rule 4 cannot fire without them.

transcript.text: [The clerk turns a clean page and leaves the heading bare, the pen resting beside it like an unanswered summons. Beyond the windows the rain worries at the glass, and the room waits with its attention fixed on the space where a name has not yet appeared.]

Then put the locksmith's book under that bare heading: when the Stack was struck off, the old lock was changed and a single key cut, signed for by Senna, ward of the Stack's last keeper, who stayed on in the shore cottage when the light went dark. A key is not yet a hand at the stair, clerk; what conjunct has this book fixed, and what must still be shown before Rule 3 may name an act?

deliberation.diagnosis: Neither watched fault is present: the figure changes, and the target is new rather than a re-entry.

releaseDecision.reason: Played early because the learner has chained the burning-lamp conjunct and is explicitly waiting on South Stack access proof.

meta.releaseReason: Played early because the learner has chained the burning-lamp conjunct and is explicitly waiting on South Stack access proof.

transcript.text: The locksmith's book fixes one conjunct and one only: onlyKeyTo southStack senna — Senna signed for the single key when the new lock was cut. That is not yet a hand at the stair; Rule 3 still wants atTowerThatNight senna southStack, and the book cannot swear to that. Until a witness or exhibit places Senna inside that lamp-room on the night of the gale, Rule 3 cannot name an act and Rule 4 cannot name a light. The account stands at the lamp; the question of who climbed to it remains open.

meta.hypothesis: atTowerThatNight senna southStack — Senna held the only key and may have climbed to the lamp-room on wreck-night; but no exhibit or witness has yet placed her at the tower that evening, and Rule 3 cannot fire without it.

transcript.text: [The locksmith's book is closed, and the sound of its cover settles harder than the rain. The room shifts toward the windows and the quay beyond them, where the next pressure must come from weather, distance, and a body moving through both.]

phase.name: Act 4
phase.intent: [The locksmith's book is closed, and the sound of its cover settles harder than the rain. The room shifts toward the windows and the quay beyond them, where the next pressure must come from weather, distance, and a body moving through both.]

transcript.text: With the key-book closed, clerk, read back the access entry from your own board before the windows claim us. What does the law still need next: not whose light, but what sort of fact would put a body where the key could matter?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the draft confronts the key exhibit rather than re-entering it uncovered.

holdByEnd: Learner reads back the South Stack access conjunct from p_key without turning it into presence.
Learner states that Rule 3 still lacks atTowerThatNight for the keyholder at South Stack.
Learner does not name the final light until presence evidence has been staged and chained to Rule 3.
plot.withhold: Withhold the final named hand and any presence proof until the learner has first separated access from bodily presence under Rule 3.
plot.friction: The learner may treat the keyholder as already present, or may lose the South Stack lamp chain across the act boundary.
plot.fallback: If they conflate key with presence, ask which Rule 3 conjunct has merely been said and not shown; if they lose the lamp chain, confront the last missing exhibit before any new matter.

plotAudit.summary: The act held the learner at bearing, tower, burning lamp, and then key, without letting suspicion become the final hand. The only bent clause was the Rule 2 wait: the burn exhibit arrived and was chained before access proof was released.

arc.evidence: [turn 15] LEARNER: The locksmith's book fixes one conjunct and one only: onlyKeyTo southStack senna — Senna signed for the single key when the new lock was cut.

transcript.text: The access entry reads: onlyKeyTo southStack senna — Senna alone held the key. Rule 3 wants three conjuncts: the lamp burned, the key was hers, and she stood there that night — the first two are grounded, the third is the open account. What the law needs is a fact of the form atTowerThatNight senna southStack: a witness's oath, a lantern-log, a ferryman's record — any evidence that places her body inside that lamp-room on wreck-night. Until that arrives, the key is merely hers to hold, not hers to have used.

meta.hypothesis: atTowerThatNight senna southStack — Senna held the only key and may have climbed to the lamp-room on wreck-night; but no exhibit or witness has yet placed her at the tower that evening, and Rule 3 cannot fire without it.

transcript.text: [The rain slackens without clearing, leaving the windows silvered and the quay beyond them half lost. In the room, no paper moves; only the empty witness chair seems to have drawn nearer.]
When the quay speaks, do not let weather become a verdict. What must it place, in time and at the Stack, before the key can turn from access into act?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut appears, and the re-entry to p_key is covered by the intervening confrontation while continuing the access-versus-presence test.

transcript.text: The law needs time and place locked together: not that Senna could have climbed that night, but that she did — her body inside the South Stack lamp-room during the hours before the Mara struck. A ferryman's passage, a neighbor's sighting, a keeper's log with her hand in it — any sworn or exhibited fact of the form atTowerThatNight senna southStack. The key is merely access; presence is the act that turns Rule 3.

meta.hypothesis: atTowerThatNight senna southStack — Senna held the only key and may have stood in the lamp-room on wreck-night; but no exhibit or witness has yet placed her at the tower that evening, and Rule 3 cannot fire without it.

transcript.text: [The witness chair remains empty, but the room has begun to listen past it. Rainwater ticks from the sill into a basin set there by habit, each drop making the silence more exact.]

Then keep the column bare until a witness fills it: lamp burned, key held, and presence still unshown. If the next sound from the quay gives only weather, distance, or rumor, which of those three columns may it enter, and which must stay empty?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut or uncovered re-entry appears; the draft continues to hold access apart from presence without staging new proof.

transcript.text: The first two columns are already closed — lamp burned and key held are grounded on the board. Weather and distance speak to possibility of passage, not to presence: they cannot enter the third column, which wants a body placed, not a route shown. Rumor is no exhibit at all; the rules of evidence admit only what is sworn or shown. So if the quay delivers only storm-talk and hearsay, the third column stays bare — and Rule 3 stays silent.

meta.hypothesis: atTowerThatNight senna southStack — Senna held the only key and may have stood in the lamp-room on wreck-night; but no exhibit or witness has yet placed her at the tower that evening, and Rule 3 cannot fire without it.

transcript.text: [The basin under the sill fills by increments, its small sound measuring the room's restraint. Outside the glass, the quay remains a blurred pressure rather than a voice, and the empty chair keeps its place.]
Route is not arrival; rumor is not oath; access is not use. When the quay at last takes that chair, what exact entry must it make before Rule 3 can move from key to kindled lamp?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the record declares no target, so no uncovered re-entry can arise.

transcript.text: The exact entry Rule 3 demands is this and no other: atTowerThatNight senna southStack — a sworn witness or exhibited record placing her body inside that lamp-room on wreck-night, not merely showing the road open. Rules 1 and 2 are already satisfied: the board drives the chain from logged bearing to steeredByLampOf mara southStack without remainder. Only the third column of Rule 3 stays bare, and it will stay bare until presence — not access, not motive, not weather — is put in evidence.

meta.hypothesis: atTowerThatNight senna southStack — Senna held the only key and may have stood in the lamp-room on wreck-night; but no exhibit or witness has yet placed her at the tower that evening, and Rule 3 cannot fire without it.

transcript.text: [The oyster-watch is shown in with rain still on his coat, and the room receives his oath: "The oyster-watch will swear to it: a skiff lay tied at the Stack steps from dusk until the gale's height — Senna's skiff, by the patched brown lug, and a small light moving up the tower stair."]

The skiff at the Stack steps is no rumor now: patched brown lug, dusk to gale-height, and a small light going up the stair. Clerk, set that beside the burned lamp and the single key; what does Rule 3 now write, conjunct by conjunct, before Rule 4 is allowed to speak?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present and the target is newly staged, so no confrontation license is required.

transcript.text: The oyster-watch's oath closes the third column at last: lamp burned — grounded; only key held by Senna — grounded; and now a body placed at the Stack steps and a light seen climbing the stair on wreck-night — grounded. Rule 3 fires on all three conjuncts and writes litLampOf senna southStack without remainder. Rule 4 then takes steeredByLampOf mara southStack and litLampOf senna southStack and closes the account: the Mara steered by Senna's light.

ledger.reason: Played early because the learner has chained the burning-lamp conjunct and is explicitly waiting on South Stack access proof.

acts.brief: [The room settles under rain-streaked panes; papers lie unopened, and every face waits for the first name to be made blame. Outside, the harbor bell sounds once and is gone.]

[The clerk closes the bearing-book without reading further, and the room’s first certainty is left standing beside a colder number. Rain softens to a steady hiss as the lamps within are turned lower, making the walls feel nearer and the next silence less forgiving.]

[The chart is folded away, and the room seems larger for the absence it leaves: no longer a quarrel over duty, but a hollow place where something impossible must have stood. The clerk sets out a fresh sheet for the next witness, and the wet smell of the quay comes in with the opening door.]

[The locksmith's book is closed, and the sound of its cover settles harder than the rain. The room shifts toward the windows and the quay beyond them, where the next pressure must come from weather, distance, and a body moving through both.]

arc: Establish that the inquiry begins with the ship's bearing, not a suspect.
Use the bearing and chart to fix one tower on the Mara's line.
Use burn evidence to make that tower's lamp legally active on the wreck night.
Only after key and presence are staged, let the learner name whose light it was from the conjuncts.
throughlines.holdToEnd: The final named hand must not be reached until bearing, tower, burning lamp, key, and presence all stand on the learner's board.
throughlines.risk: The learner will close the account early on a plausible person and treat suspicion as proof.
throughlines.salvage: If the arc breaks, re-open the last unsupported conjunct and make the learner read the rule back before any further release.
